Product Design
→ → → →
Product Design

Structure is a decision.
Every decision is arguable.

Each project here was preceded by a structural problem — a gap between what a user arrives with and what the system was built to receive. What follows is both the work and the reasoning behind it.

01Full Build · HTML & CSS · Live Site
Botanic Elixir
Staged cognition — from affect to decision
End-to-End HTML & CSS WCAG IA
Portfolio Botanic Elixir Case Study Full process documentation — research, IA, copy, design, and build. View Case Study → Live Product Botanic Elixir — Live Site The deployed HTML/CSS product. Eight pages, fully functional. Open Site ↗
Framing the problem

Most digital systems are structured around explicit intent. Users are expected to arrive with a goal.

Experience-based environments tied to mood, focus, or social state do not follow this pattern. Users often arrive with an internal state that is difficult to articulate — a vague sense of need, or a misalignment between how they feel and the options typically presented.

The problem is not navigation in the traditional sense. It is translation: how does a system help a user move from a pre-verbal internal condition into a structured decision without forcing premature categorization?

Structural objective

The system is designed to support a specific cognitive sequence:

  • Affect — feeling
  • Recognition — identifying the feeling
  • Evaluation — comparing options
  • Decision

Most interfaces collapse these steps into one, introducing evaluation too early. This increases cognitive load and often results in disengagement. The structure here deliberately separates each stage.

Key decisions

Entry — Full-bleed imagery, minimal text. At this stage, the user's attention is unstable and intent has not yet formed. Providing structured information forces early analytical processing. The interface uses high-contrast imagery and reduced textual density to stabilize attention before choice is introduced.

Mood layer — Photographic imagery is used over icons or labels because affective processing is faster than symbolic processing. It allows alignment between internal state and external representation without translation effort.

Equal weight — No hierarchy, no defaults. This removes implied system preference and shifts the locus of decision-making from the system to the user.

Colour continuity — State encoding across pages. Its function is not aesthetic — it encodes prior decisions, reduces memory load, and maintains continuity of experience without requiring the user to recall their path.

Dual interaction model

The system supports two distinct cognitive approaches:

  • Intuitive users proceed through recognition with minimal friction
  • Analytical users require explanation and validation before committing

The structure accommodates both without privileging one. Depth content is available but does not interrupt the primary path.

Outcome: a staged progression through perception, recognition, evaluation, and decision — each phase isolated and supported.

Entry — perceptual stabilization before choice
Mood selection — recognition task, not decision task
Why photographs
Affective processing is faster than symbolic. Images allow internal state and external option to align without translation effort.
Why no metadata here
Introducing price or duration at this stage shifts the task from recognition to evaluation — collapsing the staged sequence the architecture is built on.
Why equal weight
Hierarchy implies system preference. This step requires the user to reference their own internal state — not match to a recommendation.
Experiences — evaluation introduced only after state is set

Colour bars function as persistent state markers. The user does not need to recall their selection — it is encoded in the visual layer across pages.

System depth — trust construction for analytical users

Dense content is intentional. It signals credibility and deliberation. The system supports two cognitive approaches without forcing users into one.

Community — recurring structure for returning users

Eight pages, deployed. Full HTML and CSS from scratch.  View live ↗

02Brand System · Figma
Cognitive Atelier Style Guide
A system that teaches its own logic
Color System Typography WCAG Figma
Figma · Brand System Cognitive Atelier Style Guide Full color palette, typography scale, accessibility specs, and graphic principles — annotated with reasoning. Open in Figma ↗
Framing the problem

Design systems that present outcomes without reasoning can be replicated, but not extended.

When a designer can only copy a decision — not understand why it was made — they cannot adapt it when conditions change. The system becomes a constraint rather than a tool.

Structural objective

The guide externalizes the decision-making process. Each element — color, typography, spacing — is accompanied by its functional role, its constraints, and its implications.

The distinction is between a prescriptive system and a generative one. This guide is built to support contextual decision-making, not imitation.

Cognitive intent
  • Reduced decision fatigue for the designer using it
  • Increased confidence in application
  • Clarity about why choices are made, not just what they are

Outcome: the system functions as a constraint framework, a decision-support tool, and a transferable logic structure.

03Redesign · Figma
DOCR Coffee Redesign
Shifting the interpretive burden from user to structure
Redesign Accessibility IA Figma
Figma · Redesign DOCR Coffee Redesign Card system, visual hierarchy corrections, and WCAG accessibility fixes — with semantic HTML spec alongside each component. Open in Figma ↗
Framing the problem

When content is dense but unstructured, the burden of interpretation shifts to the user. Most users do not perform this work.

The result is abandonment, misinterpretation, or incomplete understanding — not because the content is bad, but because the structure cannot support it.

Card system reasoning

Cards function as cognitive containers. Each answers three questions: what is this, who is it for, what does it require.

Scanning a card grid is comparison. Reading a wall of text is just reading. The structure shifts the experience from reading to deciding.

The redesign reduces working memory load, parsing effort, and ambiguity. Users move from exposure to understanding without constructing meaning themselves.

Accessibility as structure

Contrast ratios corrected to WCAG AA. Semantic HTML specified alongside every visual component. Accessibility decisions were made structurally, not added as a final layer.

Outcome: the redesign shifts the experience from reading to decision-making.

04Interaction Design · Figma
Micro-Interactions
Minimum motion, maximum signal
Motion Interaction Figma
Figma · Interaction Design Micro-Interactions Study Analysis of four button patterns plus the original typing-reveal design, with timing rationale and behavioral annotation. Open in Figma ↗
Framing the problem

Interfaces must communicate system state continuously. When they fail to, users experience uncertainty, delays feel longer, and trust decreases.

The typing-reveal

Characters appear at one-second intervals on page load. The interaction communicates three things: system readiness, availability of content, and acknowledgement of user presence.

Compared to spinners or progress bars, this interaction provides meaning rather than abstract feedback. It aligns with a familiar pattern — text appearing — and reduces perceived waiting time.

Analysis criteria

Four existing button patterns were evaluated against a single question: does this motion communicate something specific? If not, it is noise — and noise increases cognitive load.

Outcome: the interaction supports clarity of system state, reduced uncertainty, and increased perceived responsiveness.

05Additional Work
Prototypes, personas, spatial design
05a
Prototype · Figma Make
Speech-to-Text App
Accessibility & multimodal interaction
Accessibility Prototype Figma Make
Figma Make · Interactive Prototype Speech-to-Text App Real-time transcription interface with dual-channel display and accessibility-first navigation. Interact directly in the browser. Open Prototype ↗
Framing the problem

Most systems treat multimodal interaction as hierarchical — one primary mode, one fallback.

This fails users who rely on both simultaneously, requiring them to choose between channels rather than use both.

Structural objective

Audio and text are treated as equally primary channels. The interface supports parallel processing, channel validation, and synchronization — without forcing prioritization.

Screen reader navigation and dual-channel transcription research both identified the same failure mode: systems assuming one dominant modality exclude users who cannot use it. This prototype is the structural response.

05b
Research Artifact · Figma
User Personas
Apple Watch study · Synthesis
Synthesis Figma
Figma · Research Artifact User Personas — Janet, James, Emily Three personas synthesized from Apple Watch user interviews. Built to function as decision filters, not descriptions. Open in Figma ↗
Framing the problem

Personas often include biographical detail that does not inform design decisions.

When a persona includes information that doesn't narrow the possibility space, it isn't functioning as a design tool — it's functioning as a description.

Structural objective

Include only what directly impacts language, structure, and interaction decisions. Effective personas reduce decision complexity by narrowing possibilities, clarifying user needs, and guiding trade-offs.

Janet, James, and Emily preceded all layout decisions for Botanic Elixir and the health system. They function as decision filters, not descriptive artifacts.

05c
Experience Design · Figma
Serendipitous Social Space
Adaptive environment · spatial design
Social Systems Spatial Design Figma
Figma · Experience Design Serendipitous Social Space Digital social environment translating environmental psychology principles into interface structure — proximity, visibility, low-friction entry. Open in Figma ↗
Framing the problem

Serendipity cannot be designed directly. It can only be supported through structural conditions.

Key conditions
  • Proximity
  • Partial visibility
  • Low-friction entry
  • Ambient awareness

These conditions reduce social risk and increase the likelihood of low-friction interaction. They support passive engagement before active participation — the same structural logic that makes a good café also makes a good social environment online.
